source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
The Kangaroo Hotel has 30 floors numbered from 1 to 30 and each floor has 20 rooms numbered from 1 to 20. The code to enter the room is formed by joining the floor number with the room number, in that order. But this code can be confusing, as shown in the picture. Note that the code 101 is not confusing, as it can only refer to floor 10 and room 1 and never to floor 1 and room 1, as it has the code 11. How many codes are confusing, including the one in the figure?
<image1>
extraction_notes: Question explicitly refers to the picture/figure and includes an image placeholder.
Used the value from the image field; omitted decoded_image because it is not a path/URL.

image_dependency: question explicitly says 'as shown in the picture'
image_manifest contains 1 existing image and registry_passed=true
the figure appears to provide the example of a confusing code, though the core constraints are also stated in text
multi_step: solving requires reasoning over two bounded variables: floor numbers 1-30 and room numbers 1-20
the task is to identify when one concatenated code can map to more than one floor-room pair
this involves at least multiple logical steps: parse code structure, detect ambiguity conditions, and count all valid ambiguous cases
question_text is detailed and no answer choices are provided, indicating open-ended structured reasoning
verifiability: answer_type is numeric and answer_text is a single number
all constraints are explicit and finite (30 floors, 20 rooms), enabling direct enumeration or algebraic checking
the final result can be independently verified by a deterministic checker

risk_flags: image may be illustrative rather than strictly necessary because most problem constraints are stated in text

decision_reasons: asset set is complete: question text, answer, and primary image are all present with no registry issues
initial scoring is strong overall, with high image dependency, multi-step reasoning, and verifiability scores
the answer is numeric and the problem constraints are explicit and finite, making the sample easy to verify
the only noted risk is that the image may be partly illustrative, which does not undermine the sample's recoverability or overall quality
